sources.title: Directive Ashes of Peace Campaign v0.2

areas.name: Bridge
areas.exteriorPlacement: Dorsal crown of the primary hull
hardFacts: The bridge, captain's ready room, and briefing room are on Deck 1.
The bridge is an ovoid, multi-level command space rather than a flat cockpit.
constraints: Bridge prose should turn distributed ship activity into accountable command decisions.

areas.name: Captain's ready room and briefing room
areas.exteriorPlacement: Dorsal primary hull near the bridge
sceneUses: transition back to public bridge authority
hardFacts: The ready room and briefing room sit adjacent to the Deck 1 bridge.
constraints: Private command scenes should retain the pressure that the bridge is only a few steps away.

areas.name: Mess hall and galley
areas.exteriorPlacement: Forward primary hull with forward-facing windows
hardFacts: The mess hall is on Deck 2 and functions as a forward-facing social commons.
constraints: The mess hall is a community workspace, not a restaurant or grand lounge.

areas.name: Captain's quarters
hardFacts: The captain's quarters are Deck 3 baseline, larger than standard quarters but still compact.
constraints: If quarters become a second ready room, that should signal command strain.

areas.name: Transporter rooms
hardFacts: Deck 4 contains transporter-room references; standard rooms use pads and an operator station.
constraints: Transporters should not erase interference, shields, range, quarantine, or biofilter risk.

areas.name: Sickbay
hardFacts: Sickbay is on Deck 5 with the main ward, CMO office, medical lab, surgical capability, and EMH support.
constraints: Sickbay should expose consequences without turning medical care into exposition.

areas.name: Holodecks
hardFacts: The Intrepid-class baseline includes two holodecks on Deck 6.
constraints: Holodecks are not limitless rooms, power sources, or plot escapes.

areas.name: Armory and security suite
areas.zone: Deck 6 armory baseline and ship-specific security spaces
hardFacts: The Deck 6 baseline includes armory references; brig and security office placement should be package-authored when exactness matters.
constraints: Security spaces should keep Starfleet accountability visible when force enters a scene.

areas.name: Astrometrics and science labs
hardFacts: Astrometrics and science-lab references sit on Deck 8 baseline.
constraints: Astrometrics should make models useful without making them automatically complete truth.

areas.name: Crew quarters
hardFacts: Crew quarters are distributed across multiple inhabited decks; captain's quarters use the Deck 3 baseline.
constraints: Quarters are private spaces inside a non-private machine.

areas.name: Main engineering
areas.zone: Deck 11 warp core and systems integration space
hardFacts: Main engineering is on Deck 11 and centers on a two-level room around the warp core.
constraints: Engineering should name accepted risk, temporary workaround, pending verification, and unresolved fault as different things.

areas.name: Deflector control and forward engineering support
areas.exteriorPlacement: Internal support for the forward main deflector
hardFacts: Deflector work should connect the external dish to internal control rooms, power cost, and hazard risk.
constraints: A deflector trick must cost power, emitters, field stability, or repair margin.

areas.name: Cargo bays
hardFacts: Cargo Bay 2 has Deck 4 and Deck 8 references; package-specific placement should be used carefully.
constraints: Cargo bays feel empty until needed, then become too small for the crisis.

areas.name: Shuttlebay 1 and shuttlebay 2
areas.exteriorPlacement: Aft dorsal secondary hull between the swept nacelle-pylon frame
hardFacts: The main shuttlebay complex is aft on Deck 10.
Small craft approach from astern toward the aft bay.
Shuttlebay 1 is the primary flight deck; the larger maintenance hangar/shuttlebay 2 sits behind it.
Ashes opening exception: the player's arrival uses Shuttlebay 2, not Shuttlebay 1.
constraints: Do not depict routine Intrepid-class shuttle docking through a wide mouth in the underside of the saucer.
Launch and recovery involve shields, force fields, tractor/arresting support, and flight-deck clearance.
For the Ashes opening player arrival, do not land, lower, or berth the shuttle on Shuttlebay 1; use Shuttlebay 2.

areas.name: Jefferies tubes and maintenance access
hardFacts: Jefferies tubes and maintenance access exist across multiple decks and expose the ship's physical infrastructure.
constraints: Tube scenes should use physical constraint rather than generic corridor action.

areas.name: Lower-deck support spaces
hardFacts: Lower decks contain support spaces that can decide whether command authority, life support, or power routing survives.
constraints: Low-glamour support spaces should matter when bridge abstractions become physical failures.

systems.name: Bio-neural gel-pack network
systems.capability: Fast integrated processing across critical and support systems.

systems.name: Variable-geometry nacelles
systems.capability: Nacelles angle for warp and give the class a visible mechanical transition.

systems.name: Emergency Medical Hologram infrastructure
systems.capability: Mark I EMH support extends medical readiness when staff are absent, overwhelmed, or incapacitated.

systems.name: Transporter systems
systems.capability: Transporters compress away-team movement into seconds when locks, shields, biofilters, and power permit.

systems.name: Holodeck power and simulation
systems.capability: Holodecks can simulate missing spaces, training hazards, therapy settings, and morale pressure valves.

systems.name: Shields, phasers, torpedoes, and tractor beams
systems.capability: A capable multi-mission Starfleet tactical suite, not a pure warship default.

systems.name: Replicators, stores, and compact logistics
systems.scope: Shipboard supplies, mission stores, and replicator policy
systems.capability: Replicators and cargo stores make the ship generous but still finite under relief, repair, medical, or mission pressure.

systems.name: Blue alert and planetary landing systems
systems.scope: Atmospheric entry, landing, and grounded ship posture
systems.capability: The class can land on a planetary surface, but the ship becomes a load-bearing object in gravity.

payload.summary: The Breckenridge is an Intrepid-class Starfleet vessel: compact, advanced, fast, and built for autonomous long-range operations without Galaxy-class scale.
constraints: Use compact capability and recurring ship spaces rather than generic starship sprawl.
Voyager is the class model, not imported campaign history.
hardAnchors: 15-deck Intrepid-class baseline.
Small crew scale around a close shipboard community.

payload.summary: The exterior reads fast and compact: arrowhead primary hull, tucked secondary hull, forward deflector, swept pylons, and variable-geometry nacelles.
constraints: Exterior descriptions should respect hull placement, windows, nacelles, deflector, and bay geometry.
hardAnchors: Variable-geometry nacelles are a visible warp-readiness tell.
The primary hull is an arrowhead saucer, not a broad circular city.
avoid: Exterior views that ignore where rooms and shuttle doors are on the hull.

payload.summary: Normal shuttle approach to an Intrepid-class ship is aft, toward the Deck 10 shuttlebay complex in the dorsal secondary hull, visually framed by the nacelle-pylon geometry.
constraints: Do not depict routine shuttle docking through the underside, belly, ventral saucer, or primary-hull mouth.
Use aft approach lights, bay force field, arresting support, and deck crew for shuttle recovery texture.
For the Ashes opening player arrival, Shuttlebay 2 is the arrival bay; do not land, lower, or berth the player's shuttle on Shuttlebay 1.
hardAnchors: Deck 10 aft shuttlebay complex.
Small craft approach from astern.
Shuttlebay 1 is the primary flight deck; shuttlebay 2 is the larger maintenance hangar behind it.
Ashes opening player arrival uses Shuttlebay 2, not Shuttlebay 1.
avoid: wide lit mouth in the underside of the saucer

payload.summary: The Deck 1 bridge is an ovoid, multi-level command room where conn, ops, tactical, science, and command turn shipwide activity into decisions.
constraints: Do not write the bridge as an empty cockpit; use stations, watch rhythm, and public command posture.
hardAnchors: Deck 1 bridge with ready room and briefing room adjacent.

cards.title: Deck 1 ready room and briefing room

refs: 5.2 Captain's ready room and briefing room, Deck 1

payload.summary: The ready room and briefing room sit beside the Deck 1 bridge, letting command move quickly between public orders and private judgment.
constraints: Private scenes here should retain the awareness that the bridge is one door away.
hardAnchors: Deck 1 adjacent command spaces.

payload.summary: The Deck 2 forward mess hall is the ship's social commons, where meals, morale, informal diplomacy, memorials, and overflow care can all happen.
constraints: Write it as a lived-in crew commons, not a restaurant or luxury lounge.
hardAnchors: Deck 2 forward social center.

payload.summary: Deck 4 transporter rooms are mission thresholds: six-pad platform, operator focus, biofilter judgment, and the risk that routine transport becomes identity or quarantine crisis.
constraints: Transporters need sensor locks, pattern integrity, shield status, biofilters, and operator judgment.
hardAnchors: Deck 4 transporter facilities.

payload.summary: Deck 5 sickbay contains the main ward, biobeds, surgical capability, CMO office, medical lab, isolation capacity, and EMH support.
constraints: Use medical authority and visible consequences; do not let command prose erase injury, triage, quarantine, or return-to-duty limits.
hardAnchors: Deck 5 sickbay.
CMO office and EMH support are part of the medical suite.

cards.title: Deck 6 holodecks, armory, and security pressure

refs: 5.8 Armory and security suite, Deck 6 baseline

payload.summary: Deck 6 baseline includes holodecks and armory references: simulation can reveal morale or train repairs, while security spaces keep force accountable.
constraints: Holodecks are not unlimited plot escapes; armory and brig scenes should keep procedure and chain of custody visible.
hardAnchors: Two holodecks on Deck 6 baseline.
Armory references are Deck 6 baseline.

payload.summary: Deck 8 astrometrics and science labs turn space into navigable models, sensor confidence, route risk, and scientific disagreement.
constraints: A beautiful model is not complete truth; include confidence, raw reading, corrected reading, and operational consequence when relevant.
hardAnchors: Deck 8 astrometrics baseline.

refs: 5.12 Deflector control and forward engineering support, Deck 11 baseline

payload.summary: Deck 11 main engineering is a two-level room around the warp core, with catwalk, chief engineer's office, open work area, power distribution, and nearby deflector-control pressure.
constraints: Engineering turns command desire into cost; name power tradeoffs, accepted risk, temporary workaround, pending verification, or unresolved fault.
hardAnchors: Main engineering is on Deck 11.
Deflector support/control belongs to the Deck 11 forward engineering support baseline.

refs: 5.13 Cargo bays, Deck 4 and Deck 8 references

payload.summary: Cargo bays are adaptable volume for stores, mission equipment, relief supplies, quarantine, temporary shelter, and improvised facilities.
constraints: Inventory should matter; replicators do not erase rare parts, medical limits, relief arithmetic, or cargo evidence.
hardAnchors: Cargo Bay 2 has Deck 4 and Deck 8 references; use exact placement carefully.

payload.summary: The Deck 10 aft shuttlebay complex is the ship's mission threshold: shuttlebay 1 flight deck, larger maintenance hangar/shuttlebay 2 behind it, deck crew, EVA lockers, and shuttle-control work. In the Ashes opening, the player's arrival uses Shuttlebay 2, not Shuttlebay 1.
constraints: Do not place normal Intrepid-class shuttlebay doors in the underside of the saucer.
Use shields, force fields, approach lights, arresting support, flight-deck clearance, and medical/security standby when recovery is risky.
For the Ashes opening player arrival, do not land, lower, or berth the shuttle on Shuttlebay 1; use Shuttlebay 2.
hardAnchors: Deck 10 aft dorsal secondary hull.
Approach from astern.
Shuttlebay 1 primary flight deck; shuttlebay 2 larger maintenance hangar.
Ashes opening player arrival uses Shuttlebay 2, not Shuttlebay 1.

payload.summary: Jefferies tubes and lower-deck support spaces turn the sleek ship into a physical body of hot panels, ladder rungs, relays, environmental control, and hard-to-reach failures.
constraints: Use cramped physical risk and unglamorous dependencies rather than generic corridor action.
hardAnchors: Maintenance access crosses multiple decks; lower decks hold support spaces that can become mission-critical.

payload.summary: Bio-neural gel packs make the Intrepid class fast and integrated, but failures can behave like contamination or cascade instead of a simple broken circuit.
constraints: Gel packs are advanced components with maintenance and supply constraints, not proof the ship is sentimentally alive.
hardAnchors: Shipwide bio-neural processing can link local symptoms across departments.

payload.summary: Variable-geometry nacelles angle into warp posture, giving the class a visible readiness tell and a pylon-based vulnerability.
constraints: Use nacelle motion for warp transition, damage, inspection, landing posture, and external approach scenes rather than as a constant gimmick.
hardAnchors: Nacelle/pylon posture is part of Intrepid-class exterior truth.

payload.summary: An Intrepid-class ship can land, but blue alert is a major shipwide posture: landing stations, structural-integrity watch, thruster balance, secured small craft, and deck-plate stress.
constraints: Do not treat landing like casual parking; use shuttles, transporters, orbit, or docking when simpler.
hardAnchors: Planetary landing is a major operational event.

cards.title: Transporters, EMH, holodecks, tactical systems, and logistics

refs: 6.6 Shields, phasers, torpedoes, and tractor beams

payload.summary: The ship's support systems are strong but bounded: EMH coverage depends on emitters and computer integrity; transporters need locks and biofilters; tactical systems have arcs and inventories; stores and replicators still create logistics.
constraints: Do not use advanced systems to erase every logistical, ethical, power, or quarantine problem.
hardAnchors: Advanced systems create dependencies and command obligations.
